summary: Soaring Terrapin Station jam and tight first‑set classics
blurb: The show delivers a towering Terrapin Station performance followed by an explosive post‑Terrapin jam that flows into I Will Take You Home, Wharf Rat and Throwing Stones. Jerry’s guitar is solid and energetic while Phil provides a driving bass line and celebrates his birthday with the crowd. Brent’s keyboards soar throughout, making this a must‑listen for spring ’90 fans.
review: On March 15, 1990 at the Capital Centre, the Dead rode a wave of high energy that culminated in one of the most memorable Terrapin Station outings of the spring ’90 run. The first set was tight and fire‑filled, highlighted by “Easy To Love You,” “Just Like Tom Thumb's Blues,” and a punchy “Althea” that showcased Jerry’s soaring fills and Bob’s gritty slide work. Phil’s bass shone on “Tom Thumb's Blues” and his birthday shout‑out at the start of the second set lifted the crowd into a seamless jam that stretched Terrapin Station into a post‑Terrapin improvisation, then rolled through “I Will Take You Home,” “Wharf Rat,” and “Throwing Stones” before closing with a rousing “Revolution.” Brent Mydland’s haunting keyboards added an extra layer of bliss, especially on the Terrapin jam and the piano‑driven “I Will Take You Home.” The rhythm section—Mickey and Bill—delivered thundering, propulsive drumming that drove the improvisations forward. Across multiple soundboard and audience sources, listeners agree this night is a high‑energy, expertly performed snapshot of the band at a creative peak, making it an essential listen for any Deadhead collection.

rating_calculation.sentiment_rationale: The majority of recordings (8 out of 9) are marked positive, repeatedly praising the Terrapin Station performance, post‑Terrapin jam, Brent’s keyboards and Phil’s birthday moment. Multiple sources use language like "epic," "soaring," "explosive" and "high‑energy," indicating strong consensus of exceptional playing across the show.

best_recording.reason: Excellent Miller soundboard capture with crisp instrument separation, no dropouts, and the full set—including the standout Terrapin Station jam—presented clearly.

key_highlights: Terrapin Station performance that expands into a post‑Terrapin jam
High‑energy first set featuring Easy To Love You and Just Like Tom Thumb's Blues
Phil Lesh’s birthday moment energizing the second‑set transition
Brent Mydland’s soaring keyboards throughout, especially on I Will Take You Home

band_performance.Jerry: Solid, energetic guitar work with soaring fills on Althea and a jammy mood that drives the second‑set improvisations.
band_performance.Phil: Driving bass that shines on Just Like Tom Thumb's Blues; vocal interplay on Easy To Love You; birthday shout‑out adds excitement to the set transition.
band_performance.Bob: Standout slide work on Althea, strong rhythm guitar throughout, and spirited vocals on Revolution.
band_performance.Brent: Haunting, soaring keyboards that elevate Terrapin Station and I Will Take You Home; consistently praised as ‘on fire’ all night.
band_performance.Mickey: Powerful, thundering drumming that propels the post‑Terrapin jam and adds weight to the Rhythm Devils section.
band_performance.Billy: Dynamic, driving drums complement Mickey’s power, together delivering a thunderous backbone for the improvisations.
